section: Chapter 8: A Shirt in the Market

concepts.lesson: A shirt goes through several stages before reaching the market. These stages include growing cotton, spinning yarn, weaving cloth, stitching garments, and selling the finished product.
concepts.real_example: Cotton grown by farmers is sent to spinning mills where it is turned into yarn and later stitched into shirts in garment factories.
key_points: Many stages involved in making a shirt
Final product reaches customers through markets

concepts.lesson: Cotton farmers grow cotton which serves as the raw material for making cloth and garments. Farmers sell cotton to traders or mills for further processing.
concepts.real_example: Farmers in Gujarat and Maharashtra grow cotton that is later used by textile factories.
key_points: Cotton is the main raw material
Farmers are the first step in production
Raw materials start the production chain

concepts.lesson: After cotton is harvested, it is sent to spinning mills where it is converted into yarn. This yarn is then woven into cloth in weaving units or textile factories.
concepts.real_example: Textile mills convert cotton into yarn and weave it into fabric that is used for making shirts and other garments.
key_points: Factories and mills play an important role

concepts.lesson: Garment factories cut, stitch, and assemble cloth into finished shirts. Workers in these factories perform specialized tasks such as cutting, stitching, and finishing.
concepts.real_example: Garment workers in cities like Tiruppur stitch thousands of shirts for both domestic and export markets.
key_points: Factories produce large quantities of clothing

concepts.lesson: Traders and middlemen buy raw materials from farmers and sell them to factories or export companies. They connect producers with markets.
concepts.real_example: A cotton trader purchases cotton from farmers and supplies it to textile mills.
key_points: They help goods move through the production chain
They often earn profit by buying and selling

concepts.lesson: Large companies often subcontract work to smaller factories or workshops. These smaller units produce goods according to the requirements of big companies.
concepts.real_example: Export companies give stitching work to small garment units that employ local workers.

concepts.lesson: Workers involved in garment production often receive low wages compared to the high prices at which shirts are sold in the market.
concepts.real_example: Garment workers may stitch many shirts daily but receive only small daily wages.
key_points: Income inequality exists in the production chain

concepts.lesson: Many shirts produced in garment factories are exported to international markets. Global demand influences production, pricing, and employment.
concepts.real_example: Indian garment factories export shirts to countries in Europe and the United States.
key_points: Export companies connect local production with global trade

concepts.lesson: Different participants in the production chain receive different shares of profit. Large companies and brands often earn the highest profits compared to farmers and workers.
concepts.real_example: A branded shirt sold in a mall costs much more than the amount paid to the worker who stitched it.
key_points: Brands and retailers earn larger margins
Workers and farmers earn smaller shares

concepts.lesson: The production chain describes the sequence of people and processes involved in making and selling a shirt, from raw materials to the final customer.
concepts.real_example: Cotton Farmer → Trader → Spinning Mill → Weaving Unit → Garment Factory → Retail Shop → Customer.
